cards.front: How do possessive pronouns (mein/dein) change by case and gender?
cards.answer:
Possessive pronouns take the SAME endings as the indefinite article 'ein' in each case.
Masculine: mein (Nom) / meinen (Akk) / meinem (Dat) / meines+s (Gen)
Feminine: meine / meine / meiner / meiner
Neuter: mein / mein / meinem / meines+s
Plural: meine / meine / meinen+n / meiner
This applies to mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, Ihr — only the stem changes, the endings are always the same.
cards.note: Memory trick: possessive endings mirror 'ein-words' (kein, mein, dein...). If you know how 'kein' declines, you know them all.
cards.code:
Nom: mein / meine / mein / meine
Akk: meinen / meine / mein / meine
Dat: meinem / meiner / meinem / meinen(n)
Gen: meines(s) / meiner / meines(s) / meiner
cards.example: Ich fahre mit meinem Bruder. (I'm traveling with my brother.)
cards.application: Talking about family or belongings in the ÖSD A2 speaking exam (e.g. 'in meiner Familie').
prompts: Same pattern as which article type?
cards.cloze: Ich wohne mit ___ Familie. (I live with ___ family.) [feminine, Dativ]

cards.front: Why is it 'in meiner Familie' and not 'in meinem Familie'?
cards.answer:
Two rules combine here:
1) 'Familie' is feminine (die Familie).
2) 'in' is a two-way preposition (Wechselpräposition) — used with Dativ when describing a static location/state (no movement), Akkusativ when describing movement toward something.
'Wie viele Personen sind in deiner Familie?' describes a state, not motion, so Dativ is used. Feminine + Dativ = the ending -er, giving 'meiner'.
cards.note: Chain the logic: state (not motion) → Dativ → feminine noun → '-er' ending.
cards.example: Wir sind vier Personen in meiner Familie. (There are four people in my family.)
cards.application: Answering 'Wie viele Personen sind in deiner Familie?' in the ÖSD A2 speaking exam.
cards.cloze: Wie viele Personen sind in ___ Familie? (How many people are in ___ family?)

cards.front: What are Wechselpräpositionen and how do you choose Dativ vs. Akkusativ?
cards.answer:
Nine prepositions can take either Dativ or Akkusativ depending on meaning: in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen.
Rule: 'Wohin?' (movement toward a place) → Akkusativ. 'Wo?' (location/state, no movement) → Dativ.
Test: does the sentence describe going somewhere (motion) or being/staying somewhere (state)?
cards.note: Mnemonic: 'Wohin' has motion built into the word (hin = toward) → Akkusativ. 'Wo' alone = just location → Dativ.
cards.code:
9 prepositions: in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen
Wo? (state) -> Dativ
Wohin? (motion) -> Akkusativ
cards.example: Ich bin in der Schule. (I am at school.) vs. Ich gehe in die Schule. (I am going to school.)
cards.application: Describing where you live, work, or study during the ÖSD A2 speaking section.
cards.cloze: Das Buch liegt auf ___ Tisch. (The book is lying on ___ table.) [state, Dativ]

cards.front: Why does 'der' sometimes appear with feminine nouns, like 'in der Schule'?
cards.answer:
'der' is not only the masculine Nominativ article — it also appears as:
1) Feminine Dativ (in der Schule)
2) Feminine Genitiv (das Auto der Frau)
3) Plural Genitiv (der Kinder)
The gender of a noun never changes; only the article's form changes based on case. Seeing 'der' does not automatically mean masculine — check the case and gender together.
cards.note: Don't pattern-match 'der = masculine'. Always ask: which case, which gender?
cards.example: Ich bin in der Schule. (I am at school.) — feminine Dativ, not masculine.
cards.application: Avoiding a common beginner mistake when using 'in + die-nouns' in Dativ.
prompts: Name 2 other uses of 'der' besides masc. Nominativ?
cards.cloze: Ich bin in ___ Küche. (I am in ___ kitchen.) [feminine, Dativ]

cards.front: zuhören vs. hören — why does zuhören take Dativ (mir) instead of Akkusativ (mich)?
cards.answer:
'hören' (to hear) is a normal verb taking Akkusativ: 'Du hörst mich nicht' (You don't hear me).
'zuhören' (to listen to) is a separate, separable verb (zu + hören) that grammatically requires Dativ, regardless of Turkish/English logic: 'Sie hören mir nicht zu' (You're not listening to me/to me).
Dativ-requiring verbs must be memorized individually; they don't always match the direct-object logic of Turkish or English.
cards.note: Contrastive: Turkish 'beni dinlemiyorsun' suggests direct object (Akkusativ) logic, but German zuhören is grammatically Dativ. Compare to helfen, which happens to match Turkish 'sana' = Dativ.
cards.example: Hör mir bitte zu! (Please listen to me!)
cards.application: Avoiding a very common Turkish-speaker mistake (using mich instead of mir) with zuhören.
prompts: One example verb that DOES match Turkish logic?
cards.cloze: Ich höre ___ nicht zu. (I'm not listening to ___.) [dative pronoun]

cards.front: Which common verbs require Dativ, and how does that compare to Turkish?
cards.answer:
Dativ-requiring verbs: helfen (to help), zuhören (to listen to), danken (to thank), gefallen (to please/like), gehören (to belong to).
Some match Turkish possessive/dative logic (helfen -> sana yardım ediyorum = 'to you'), others don't (zuhören sounds like 'seni' in Turkish but is Dativ in German). These must be memorized as a list rather than derived from Turkish translation.
cards.note: Group them as a set and drill them together rather than translating word by word from Turkish.
cards.code: helfen (yardım etmek), zuhören (dinlemek), danken (teşekkür etmek), gefallen (hoşuna gitmek), gehören (ait olmak) — all take Dativ
cards.example: Das gefällt mir sehr. (I like that a lot / that pleases me.)
cards.application: Common everyday phrases usable in the ÖSD A2 speaking exam.
cards.cloze: Ich danke ___ für die Hilfe. (I thank ___ for the help.) [dative pronoun]
